data.title: FBL: Strategic Tool For Active Traders Looking To Amplify Meta Gains
data.description:
Introduction

The GraniteShares 2x Long META Daily ETF (NASDAQ:FBL) is a leveraged fund that aims to provide investors with 200% of the daily percentage change of the underlying company, Meta. This fund is best for active traders who are aware of the risks of investing in levered funds, and who can monitor the market and Meta news to stay on top of any market activity. Please see the below image for some key facts, from the prospectus.

Additionally, the daily volume is 568K, which is strong for an ETF. Its average volume is 524K, which also signifies strong liquidity.

We like Meta as a company and in the below section will review some recent news and announcements that could be catalysts for trading FBL.

Underlying Company News

Meta reveals new smart glasses

This reveal, ahead of their annual Connect show, is a prime example of something that can move Meta stock, and therefore be a good catalyst to trade FBL with. Meta's new virtual reality product is an unreleased pair of RayBan sunglasses that come with a display and a wristband to control the user interface. This technology is possible through surface electromyography, which allows the device to interpret hand signals that can control the platform. An announcement like this, which can often drive retail hype and speculation, is a great example of a catalyst to trade FBL with. Ahead of their upcoming Connect event, which might reveal even more new products, partnerships, or news, FBL is a strong way for active traders to get exposure to Meta stock movement.

Meta's AI group is innovating and being more efficient

There was a recent release of a new, compact AI model from Meta's AI labs, called the MobileLLM R1, which is an edge reasoning model with less than 1 billion parameters, so very small by relative standards, but performs 2-5x better than comparable sized models from peers. See the below image that highlights this model's performance.

This model, according to Meta, is able to match Alibaba's Qwen3 0.6B parameter model with 8.6x less data, which is very impressive. It is also powered by 22 transformers, which are critical to the model being able to process prompts and quickly return a response. These transformers are able to take prompts, turn them into complex mathematical equations, draw relationships and conclusions, and provide an answer to the user. Meta's new model shows that the firm can innovate well in AI, and that their AI investments in hiring are starting to pay off.

Meta is continuing to innovate through partnerships in key areas like cybersecurity

The firm recently announced a new benchmarking tool using AI for enterprise cybersecurity teams, in a partnership with CrowdStrike. This platform will help security teams measure and benchmark their systems, to evaluate how prepared it may be for a real world cyber attack. This sort of testing and iterative approach to cybersecurity is critical to avoid any major hack, and these are 2 leading companies at the forefront of advanced computing and cybersecurity. We believe this is the sort of news that could move the stock as well. Further, if this is just the start of Meta getting into cybersecurity, that could spell another possibly lucrative business for the firm. We are bullish on this announcement, and believe that this is the exact type of catalyst that could be a buying opportunity on a fund like FBL.

Peer ETFs

There are 2 other ETFs that we looked at for comparison purposes for FBL. See the chart below. All figures are taken from their pages on Yahoo Finance.

ETF

Expense Ratio

YTD Performance

FBL - 2x

1.15%

37.17%

METU - 2x

1.06%

30.88%

SOCL

0.65%

42.92%

FBL outperforms its closest competitor, METU, which is another 2x leveraged fund. However, it is under par when comparing it to SOCL, which is a diversified social media ETF - which is cheaper and has returned more YTD.

Further, there are other ETFs that could benefit an investor looking for Meta exposure. These include METW for weekly options income, or METD if you are trying to play the inverse of daily Meta returns. These different ETF strategies all have different risk and reward profiles and are geared towards different strategies, so please do your own diligence before investing in any of these funds.

Risks of Leverage

When it comes to leveraged investments, there are risks associated that are not always intuitive for investors to understand.

To explore the risks of FBL, we look at the concept of loss recoupment. The idea is to understand how to breakeven from a loss. From a dollar basis, the answer is simple: if you lose $20 today, then you need to gain $20 to breakeven. On a percentage basis the answer is not so straightforward. If you lose 20% today, you need to gain 25% tomorrow to breakeven - the gain required is higher.

For most investments, the percentage based perspective is not as relevant because the actual (dollar) value of the stock is most important.

The analysis of loss recoupment for leveraged investments differs from standard investments in 2 ways:

  1. We look at the underlying security (in this case META) rather than the movement of the actual fund. This is because the performance of the fund is wholly dependent on the performance of META. So instead we ask "If I am a shareholder of FBL and META loses X today, how much does META need to increase by tomorrow for me to breakeven on FBL?"

  2. We look at the percentage basis rather than the dollar basis because FBL's performance tracks 2x the daily percentage performance of META, not the dollar performance.

The below chart compares the percentage loss on day 1 with the percentage gain required on day 2 to breakeven on a standard investment.

Not only is the gain required on day 2 always higher than the loss, but it also increases at a faster rate. This shows us that the impacts of losses (on a percentage basis) are higher than equivalent gains. Additionally, the higher the loss the larger the gap between the loss and the breakeven gain.

Next we add in a red line to signify the same relationship for leveraged investments. (Note: As we discussed, leveraged investments are analyzed from the lens of the underlying security so the loss on day 1 and the gain on day 2 correspond with the movement of META required to breakeven on FBL)

We see that the line for 2x investments is similar to the one for standard investments. The gain required is always higher and it grows faster than the loss. However we see that the red line is even higher than the blue line and grows at a much faster rate.

We conclude that the risks of leveraged investments are more magnified than for standard investments. Additionally, while on a percentage basis, losses outweigh gains, it is especially true for leveraged investments.

Note on volatility: One additional risk that stems out of this same phenomenon is volatility. If you are holding FBL over a period of time longer than 1 day and META has high volatility during this period, your corresponding gains from FBL will likely be below 2x that of METAs. It could even be a negative gain even if META has a positive gain. This is because again losses outweigh gains. So when you have a period of volatility with many up and down days, the down days will have a bigger impact and drag down your performance.

To illustrate this, let's look at 2 scenarios:

Scenario 1:

In this scenario, META increases by 19.16% over 5 days while FBL increases by 40.77% which is 2.13x.

Scenario 2:

In scenario 2, META also increased by 19.16%, but it had 2 negative days along the way. We see that in this case, FBL only increased by 35.04% which is 1.83x.

As you see, when you hold FBL for longer than 1 day, negative days for META will negatively impact your 2x performance multiplier, even when overall gains for META are positive.

To close our analysis of the risks of FBL, we quote GraniteShares:

The Fund is designed to be utilized only by knowledgeable investors who understand the potential consequences of seeking daily leveraged (2X) investment results, understand the risks associated with the use of leverage and are willing to monitor their portfolios frequently. For periods longer than a single day, the Fund will lose money if the Underlying Stock's performance is flat, and it is possible that the Fund will lose money even if the Underlying Stock's performance increases over a period longer than a single day. An investor could lose the full principal value of his/her investment within a single day.

Conclusion

We believe that Meta is a leader in AI and computing. They are innovative in their product development and showing marked increases in AI efficiency. The firm is also branching out into cybersecurity and is showing that they are aiming to compete in all facets of advanced computing on their path to artificial general intelligence. We believe that Meta should have consistent opportunities for traders to buy into FBL because of their myriad of product launches, events, partnerships, and splashy M&A rumors. These are all catalysts that could be opportunities for active, risk-tolerant traders to buy into FBL.

However we caution that inventors stay aware of the risks and act accordingly. 2x leveraged investments could quickly lose value even when the underlying security gains in value and the path towards breakeven after losing money on a leveraged investment is much harder.

FBL could be good for short-term tactical investments by knowledgeable investors. Sometimes to consider buying are near potential positive catalysts such as earnings announcements or new product launches.

data.title: Rising Yields Break The 'Unbreakable' Rally In S&P 500
data.description:
The unbreakable rally

The S&P 500 (SP500) has been rising almost vertically since the April selloff. This is what the recent uptrend looks like on long term chart - a vertical relentless rise, all green, no red. Many financial commentators branded this rally as an "unbreakable rally".

We know what happened: the S&P500 was rising relentlessly since the 2022 bottom, driven by the Gen AI theme, and then continued rising after the 2024 election cheering the Trump victory, expecting "the most pro-business" administration ever. But then, the market got shocked by the tariff agenda on the Liberation Day, questioning the pro-business agenda. After the Trump pivot on tariffs, the rally continued - violently.

Borderline recession

The stock market rallied even as the labor market weakened over the last three months, pointing to a borderline recession.

In fact, the bond market was already pricing a recession, as I warned. Specifically, in early September, after the weak August labor market report, the short-term yields (US2Y) were falling, pricing an aggressive monetary policy easing consistent with a recession. In addition, the long-term interest yields (US10Y) were falling as well. However, the 10-year yields were falling due to the falling real interest rates (as proxied by TIPS), while inflation expectations remained elevated - that was consistent with stagflation, or inflationary recession.

The Fed turned dovish

The Fed also got worried about the weakening labor market and made a significant dovish turn, signaling the beginning of interest rate cuts campaign, ignoring the elevated inflation.

The Fed's view has been that the balance of risk has shifted towards the labor market. Specifically, the Fed view is that the unemployment rate could spike if the no-fire/no-hire labor market is shocked with the increased layoffs. At the same time, the Fed view is that the tariff-induced inflation will be only temporary.

The September cut

Consistent with the messaging, the Fed cut the Federal Funds rate by 25 bps at the September FOMC meeting. In addition, the Fed signaled two additional cuts in 2025 and one more cut in 2026, to the terminal rate of 3.4% in 2026.

The Fed Chair Powell stated that this is a risk-management cut - to bring the Federal funds rate closer to the neutral rate.

So, after the September Fed cut, the macro setup is as follows:

  • The Fed cuts are positive for the stock market, because the Fed wants to prevent a recession, and thus the recessionary bear market.
  • However, the Fed is ignoring the rising inflation, referring to the long-term inflation expectations as anchored. The 10Y Break-even inflation is a proxy for inflation expectations, and it's just below the key level of 2.5%. The break above 2.5% would signal de-anchoring of inflation expectations, which would be bad news for the stock market, and could force the Fed to make a hawkish pivot.
  • However, the mortgage rates are affected by nominal interest rates, and an increase in the nominal 10Y yields would actually tighten the financial conditions, and possibly burst the housing market bubble - despite the Fed cutting short term interest rates.

Thus, it could be argued that the Fed cut actually increased the volatility in the financial markets, and the primary transmission is via long term interest rates.

Rising long term yields

So, what happened since the Fed cut interest rates?

  • The nominal 10Y yields increased from 4% to 4.18%.
  • The real yield, or 10Y TIPS, increased from 1.6 to 1.78%, thus the increase in the nominal yield was caused entirely by the increase in the real yield.
  • Inflation expectations, or 10Y BE rate, stayed unchanged at around 2.4%.

What does it mean?

The increase in the real yield suggests that the bond market is now less worried about the recession, and expects growth to pick up, that should be positive for the stock market.

At the same time, inflation expectations stayed anchored, so the pick-up in growth is not expected to be inflationary. This is also great news for the stock market. It's the goldilocks scenario.

However, despite the begin increase in nominal 10Y yields, the fact is that rising 10Y yields tighten the financial conditions. In 2024 the Fed also cut the Federal Funds rate in September, and the nominal 10Y yield kept rising until January, and forced the Fed to pause the easing cycle.

If the nominal 10Y yields keep rising, in the current episode, towards the 5% level as the Fed continues to cut interest rates, the financial conditions would significantly tighten. The risk is that eventually the 10BE inflation could break the 2.5% level, and this would force the Fed to rethink the easing campaign.

The stock market rally breaks?

The S&P500 is currently in a dip, down to the 20dma support line, which held consistently since the April bottom. We cannot infer that the rally is broken, until the 20 dma support is broken. But we are in a dip, and let's see if the 20dma support holds.

What caused the dip? How likely is the breakdown of the 20 dma support?

At this point, let's focus on the 10Y yield as the key guide, as discussed on this article. The top in the S&P 500 was September 22nd - the stock market went up after the Fed cut.

But the 10Y yields also went up after the Fed cut. On September 22nd, when the S&P 500 peaked, the 10Y yields crossed the key technical resistance level at 20 dma, as the chart below shows.

Thus, it could be concluded that a rising 10Y yield is negative for the stock market as it tightens the financial conditions. Thus, the thesis that rising 10Y yield could break the unbreakable stock market rally is valid - and the 20dma support for the S&P 500 is likely to be broken as the 10Y yield increases above 4.2%.

Implications

Long term interest rates have been gradually rising since the Fed cut on September 17th, which is tightening the financial conditions.

The stock market appears to be negatively affected by the rising long-term yields, and if the 10Y yield keeps rising, the S&P 500 is likely to have a deeper correction.

The (signaled) Fed cuts with inflation near 3% are likely to keep the pressure on 10Y yield, which should be negative for the stock market. On the other hand, the Fed's hawkish turn would also be negative for the stock market. It's a tough situation for investors.

data.title: Above The Noise: Rate Cuts, Productivity Gains, And Gold Surge
data.description:
By Brian Levit, Global Market Strategist

“Because the mail never stops! It just keeps coming and coming and coming. There’s never a letup; it’s relentless. Every day it piles up more and more and more, and you gotta get it out, but the more you get it out, the more it keeps coming in! And then the barcode reader breaks! And then, it’s Publisher’s Clearinghouse Day!” — Newman, Seinfeld

Newman was talking about the mail, but he might as well have been describing what it feels like to be a market strategist in 2025. The issues keep piling up. Then again, so have the gains in the stock market.

By now, the key messages on these issues seem to have reached investors. I know that I’ve made my appointed rounds.

  • Tariffs will result in less optimal outcomes but are unlikely to lead to recession unless policy uncertainty persists for an extended period.
  • Federal Reserve (Fed) independence is critical, but inflation expectations in the US remain contained,1 and the Fed was likely to ease anyway, regardless of pressure from the executive branch.
  • Nonfarm payroll numbers may become harder to interpret, but there are other ways to assess the health of the labor market.
  • Fiscal deficits are rising,2 but there’s still likely to be a captive audience for US debt.
  • S&P 500 Index valuations are elevated but are largely driven by the valuations of the mega-cap companies concentrated at the top of the index.3 The rest of the market doesn’t appear particularly overvalued.4 Plus, valuations have never been reliable timing tools anyway.
  • Don’t get me started on seasonality. I’m just glad I didn’t “sell in May and go away.”5

If that’s not enough, now comes the risk of a government shutdown. But like Publisher’s Clearinghouse Day, we know it’s coming every year, and we ultimately learn to ignore it.

So why is the market able to withstand this relentlessness? Growth has been slowing but not collapsing, and potentially reaccelerating in some areas.6 Earnings have generally beaten expectations.7 The Fed is cutting interest rates.

Newman once warned Jerry, “Your day of reckoning is coming.” Investors keep hearing the same warning, but a reckoning remains unlikely.

It may be confirmation bias but…

…the much-anticipated productivity gains have already started. Corporate profitability per employee has been surging.8 This likely represents a structural shift, driven by artificial intelligence, automation, and the reallocation of capital toward intangible assets. The implications for the labor market will play out over time, but it’s hard to look at the chart below and not want to be a shareholder.

Corporate profitability per employee has been surging

Corporate profitability per employee

Since you asked (part 1)

Q: How does the US stock market typically perform in the 12 to 18 months following Fed rate cuts?

A: That’s highly dependent on the state of the economy at the time of the cut.

  • There have been instances, such as 1973 and 2007, when the Fed cut rates as the economy was on the brink of a severe recession. In those cases, stock returns over the subsequent year were poor, reflecting deteriorating fundamentals.9
  • Conversely, in periods such as 1984 and 1995, the Fed eased policy without a recession following, and stock markets responded positively.10 These episodes were characterized by mid-cycle adjustments, where rate cuts helped extend the expansion rather than signal its end.

Remember, the September rate cut wasn’t the first of this cycle. The Fed began easing in September 2024, and the one-year return from that point was strong,11 with relatively strong gains coming from cyclical sectors such as financials and industrials.12 If the current environment continues to resemble past non-recessionary rate cut cycles like 1984 and 1995, then history suggests that stocks could continue to perform well.

Since you asked (part 2)

Q: Does the surge in gold prices raise concerns that investors may be losing confidence in US institutions?

A: Gold prices have been rising due to a mix of macroeconomic and geopolitical factors, including strong central bank buying, expectations of a Fed rate cut, and classic momentum-driven trading. Despite the surge in gold, broader market behavior suggests that investors haven’t lost confidence in US institutions. Treasury yields have dropped sharply since the beginning of the year, reflecting sustained demand for government bonds.13 Inflation expectations remain well anchored, indicating continued trust in the Fed and its policy credibility.14 While the US dollar has weakened in 2025,15 it’s trading only modestly below its long-term average, reinforcing the idea that the rally in gold hasn’t been driven by a loss of faith in the US economy or its financial system.

It was said

“A government shutdown would be dangerous and harmful to millions of Americans, and it is not the answer.” — US House Speaker Mike Johnson

I run the risk of this section being outdated before this hits the “newsstands.” If it means that a shutdown was averted, then I’d welcome that development. Nonetheless, I’ll provide some thoughts. If it’s not needed this time, then you can use the thoughts for the inevitable next time!

  • There have been 21 government shutdowns in US history. They’ve been resolved, on average, within eight days.16
  • Essential government functions continue to operate during shutdowns. Senior citizens and veterans continue to receive benefits. Unemployment benefits and food stamps aren’t affected. Non-essential federal workers will be furloughed, and non-essential government activities will cease.
  • The likely direct economic impact of a government shutdown is difficult to quantify, but it’s relatively small. Government spending is roughly one-fifth of the $30.3 trillion US economy; however, only about 3% is non-essential spending.17
  • The US stock market, represented by the S&P 500 Index, posted positive returns over 12 of the 21 government shutdowns. The average return during the shutdowns is 0.1%.18

Charted territory

Are we partying like it’s 1999? Is the party, as Prince sang, about to be “over, oops, out of time”? The analogy to the late 1990s tech bubble is often made, and understandably so. The market has been driven largely by tech stocks, many of which now trade at elevated valuations.19 The key difference today, however, is that these companies are generally supported by robust earnings growth.20 Comparing the disconnect between price and earnings for Cisco, a tech bellwether of the 1990s, with NVIDIA, a current leader, illustrates the point clearly (see chart below). Unlike Cisco during the bubble era, NVIDIA hasn’t experienced the same divergence between price and earnings expectations.21

Tech earnings growth example: 1990s tech bubble vs. today

Phone a friend

I’ve been getting a lot of questions about the direction of the US dollar. I reached out to Hemant Baijal, Head of Macro Alpha and Co-Head of Emerging Markets Debt for Invesco Fixed Income. Here’s a summary of Hemant’s response:

Global cycle divergence

The post-COVID-19 world has ushered in an asynchronous global economic cycle, breaking the long-standing trend of US-led synchronized growth seen since the 2008 Global Financial Crisis.

Dollar at an inflection point

The US dollar appears to be at the beginning of a structural downtrend, driven by expensive valuations, narrowing monetary policy differentials, and a large overhang of foreign-held US assets.

Policy shifts matter

Ultra-loose fiscal policy and anticipated monetary easing in the US could further pressure the dollar,22 benefiting international markets through improved credit conditions and greater central bank flexibility abroad.

Fixed income opportunities

There are likely broad-based opportunities across global fixed income markets, especially in emerging markets like Mexico and Brazil, developed markets like Europe and Japan, and in currencies.

On the road again

Let’s keep this close to home. My daily commute from a New Jersey suburb to downtown Manhattan is officially back to its full length. I’m not sharing that to earn your sympathy. I chose to live in one of the densest places on the planet. I mention it because it’s a clear sign that the return to the office is real.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 17, 2025, based on the 3-year US Treasury inflation breakeven. A breakeven inflation rate is a market-derived estimate of future inflation, calculated by comparing the yield on a standard government bond (nominal) to the yield on a Treasury Inflation-Protected Security (TIPS) of the same maturity.

  2. Source: US Department of Treasury, Aug. 31, 2025.

  3. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 17, 2025, based on the price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 Index. The median price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of the top 10 names in the S&P 500 Index is 36.5x.

  4. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 17, 2025, based on the P/E ratio of the S&P 500 Index. The median P/E ratio for the S&P 500 excluding the top 10 companies is 18.4x.

  5. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Aug. 31, 2025, based on the return of the S&P 500 Index, which advanced 15.79% from May 1, 2025–Aug. 31, 2025.

  6. Source: US Bureau of Labor Statistics and Institute for Supply Management, Aug. 31, 2025. Slowing is based on nonfarm payrolls. Reaccelerating is based on ISM Services Purchasing Managers Index, which climbed to 52 in Aug. The demarcation between growth and contraction is 50. The ISM Services PMI tracks how the US services economy is performing by surveying business leaders in areas like retail, finance, healthcare, and hospitality. It’s based on five key components, including business activity, new orders, employment, supplier deliveries, and inventories.

  7. Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 30, 2025, based on the second-quarter earnings results for the companies in the S&P 500 Index. Earnings per share grew 11.68% year-over-year, more than double the initial consensus estimate of 4%–5%.

  8. Sources: US Bureau of Economic Analysis and US Bureau of Labor Statistics, June 30, 2025, based on total US corporate profits and total US employees on nonfarm payrolls.

  9. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Aug. 31, 2025, based on the returns of the S&P 500 Index. The Fed cut rates in Aug. 1973, and over the next 12 months, the S&P 500 fell by 28.05%. The Fed cut rates in Sept. 2007, and over the next 12 months, the S&P 500 fell by 12.68%.

  10. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Aug. 31, 2025, based on the returns of the S&P 500 Index. The Fed cut rates in Sept. 1984, and over the next 12 months, the S&P 500 advanced by 14.50%. The Fed cut rates in July 1995, and over the next 12 months, the S&P 500 advanced by 16.53%.

  11. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 19, 2025, based on the returns of the S&P 500 Index. The Fed cut rates on Sept. 17, 2024, and the S&P 500 climbed by 18.65% over the following year.

  12. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 17, 2025, based on the returns of the S&P 500 Financials Sector GICS Level 1 Index and S&P 500 Industrials Sector GICS Level 1 Index.

  13. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 17, 2025, based on the yield of the 10-year US Treasury rate, which peaked at 4.79% on Jan. 14, 2025, and fell to 4.03% on Sept. 16, 2025.

  14. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 17, 2025, based on the 3-year US Treasury inflation breakeven.

  15. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 19, 2025, based on the US Dollar Index, which measures the value of the US dollar versus a trade-weighted basket of currencies. The US Dollar Index fell 9.99% from Jan. 1, 2025–Sept. 19, 2025. The Index value was 97.64 on Sept. 19, 2025, compared to an average value of 98.86 since its 1996 inception.

  16. Source: US Department of Treasury and Invesco, Aug. 31, 2025.

  17. Source: US Bureau of Economic Analysis and US Department of Treasury, Aug. 31, 2025.

  18. Source: Bloomberg L.P. and Invesco, Aug. 31, 2025, based on the returns of the S&P 500 Index. Returns calculated over the following government shutdown dates: Sept. 30–Oct. 11, 1976; Sept. 30–Oct. 13, 1977; Oct. 31–Nov. 9, 1977; Nov. 30–Dec. 9, 1977; Sept. 30–Oct. 18, 1978; Sept. 30–Oct. 12, 1979; Nov. 20–Nov. 23, 1981; Sept. 30–Oct. 2, 1982; Dec. 17–Dec. 21, 1982; Nov. 10–Nov. 14, 1983; Sept. 30–Oct. 3, 1984; Oct. 3–Oct. 5, 1984; Oct. 16–Oct. 18, 1986; Dec. 18–Dec. 20, 1987; Oct. 5–Oct. 9, 1990; Nov. 13–Nov. 19, 1995; Dec. 15, 1995–Jan. 6, 1996; Sept. 30–Oct. 17, 2013; Jan. 20–Jan. 23, 2018; Feb. 8–Feb. 9, 2018; Dec. 22, 2018–Jan. 25, 2019.

  19. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 17, 2025, based on the price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 Index. The median price-to-earnings ratio of the top 10 names in the S&P 500 Index is 36.5x.

  20. Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 30, 2025, based on the second quarter earnings growth of the top 10 companies in the S&P 500 Index.

  21. Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 30, 2025. The mention of individual stocks isn’t intended as investment advice.

  22. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Sept. 17, 2025, based on the US Dollar Index.

Important information

NA4835470

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Earnings per share (EPS) refers to a company’s total earnings divided by the number of outstanding shares.

Fixed income investments are subject to the credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

The Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) was developed by and is the exclusive property and a service mark of MSCI Inc. and Standard & Poor’s.

Fluctuations in the price of gold and precious metals may affect the profitability of companies in the gold and precious metals sector. Changes in the political or economic conditions of countries where companies in the gold and precious metals sector are located may have a direct effect on the price of gold and precious metals.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

An inflection point is an event that results in a significant positive or negative change in the progress of a company, industry, sector, economy, or geopolitical situation.

The ISM Services Purchasing Managers' Index, which is based on Institute of Supply Management surveys of non-manufacturing supply executives nationwide, monitors business activity, new orders, employment, and supplier deliveries.

Monetary easing refers to the lowering of interest rates and deposit ratios by central banks.

Purchasing Managers’ Indexes (PMI) are based on monthly surveys of companies worldwide and gauge business conditions within the manufacturing and services sectors.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

The S&P 500® Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US stock market.

The S&P 500® Financials Index GICS Level 1 Index includes stocks in the S&P 500 Index classified as financial companies based on the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) methodology. The index is market-cap weighted.

The S&P 500® Industrials Sector GICS Level 1 Index represents the industrial sector within the S&P 500 index, as defined by the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS). It includes companies involved in capital goods, commercial and professional services, and transportation.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic, and political conditions.

Many products and services offered in technology-related industries are subject to rapid obsolescence, which may lower the value of the issuers.

The price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio measures a stock’s valuation by dividing its share price by its earnings per share.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of Sept. 19, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Above the Noise: Rate cuts, productivity gains, and gold surge by Invesco US

data.title: LFGY: A Suitable Crypto-Focused Alternative To ULTY
data.description:
Overview

YieldMax Crypto Industry & Tech Portfolio Option Income ETF (NYSEARCA:LFGY) is one of the high-yielding weekly-paying ETFs available to investors. There is a growing popularity amongst these weekly paying ETFs, and LFGY looks to service this demand by harnessing the volatility of its holdings to reward investors with a massive dividend yield. LFGY is still relatively new and has an inception dating back to January of 2025. The fund has a very reasonable expense ratio of 0.99%, considering the large yield that is paid out to investors.

Looking at the performance since the fund's inception at the start of the year, we can see how LFGY's share price has fallen by 30.4%. However, distributions are so large that when including them as part of the fund's return, the total return jumps up to 9.8% over the same period. The whole appeal of LFGY is the large annual distribution rate of approximately 72.5%. Additionally, the fund pays out these distributions on a weekly basis, which makes it an enticing choice for the income-seeking investors that are okay with elevated levels of risk. However, new payouts are declared every single week, so the listed yield may be different based on when you read this article.

Similar to other YieldMax ETFs, LFGY utilizes options to generate the income needed to support this large yield. Since we're talking about a yield this large, I believe that patient investors will be able to substantially reduce their overall risk. Although I love the higher yield that this option ETF can provide, there are some vulnerabilities and risks that investors should be aware of. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that LFGY utilizes to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

Unlike many of the other YieldMax ETFs I have covered in the past, LFGY differentiates itself by operating under a different strategy. My favorite YieldMax fund, is the YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (YMAX), because it essentially pools together all of the different funds into one product. However, the underlying positions within YMAX operate a synthetic option strategy, which means that the funds within, don't actually own any common shares of the stocks they are writing options against. This can increase the overall risk of the ETF as the price is likely to be less stable over time.

LFGY is unique because it is one of the few high-yield option ETFs that actually hold common shares of the equities they are writing options against. This means that the fund operates in a similar capacity to the widely popular YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (ULTY), which I recently issued a buy rating on. The primary difference between LFGY and ULTY is the underlying selection of holdings. LFGY specifically aims to generate option income from a portfolio of companies that are exposed to the crypto markets in some capacity.

Looking at the holdings below, we can see that iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) is the largest position at 8.98% of assets. This is followed by other notable names such as Coinbase Global (COIN), Hut 8 Corp (HUT), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), Strategy (MSTR), and Riot Platforms (RIOT) just to name a few. Despite LFGY actually holding common shares of these stocks, I suggest that investors limit their expectations around the ETF's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation over time. LFGY is best utilized for income generation above anything else.

It makes sense for LFGY to focus on these crypto-related companies because the strategy needs volatility to generate income. The higher the implied volatility of a holding, the higher the option premiums that can be collected. Although I consider these riskier positions from a growth standpoint, investors are being rewarded with high levels of income for this risk. Although I am bullish on Bitcoin (BTC-USD), I am not as bullish on these speculative companies that utilize Bitcoin.

To limit this downside risk, LFGY sells collars and put spreads. This basically means that the fund now pairs the traditional call writing with also buying puts or structured put spreads to act as insurance against the underlying stocks. This helps protect against price drops below a certain strike price. While it can soften the impact of declines, it will not stop them completely. The tradeoff of option writing is that upside movement is sacrificed to the selected strike price. Since LFGY also includes some downside protection with the collars and put spreads, the overall net yield is slightly reduced in exchange.

Performance & Risks

Despite LFGY holding shares of the stocks it is writing options against, investors should not expect the performance to track its underlying positions. Just as an example, I pulled in the performance of LFGY, including all distributions, against the performance of its top positions. As we can see below, LFGY underperforms nearly all of the listed positions, with the exception of MSTR. Therefore, investors need to understand that a high yield doesn't automatically translate to a high return. If you are bullish on any of the underlying positions, you would be better off buying those individual positions instead of LFGY.

Therefore, LFGY presents the risk of underperformance over a longer time frame. The large distributions are likely to be offset by the share price deteriorating over time. Since the use of options limits the level of capital appreciation that can be experienced, LFGY will have trouble recovering its share price over time. Even if markets experience a strong rally, YieldMax funds aren't typically known for their ability to participate in these rallies. Even if they do, the capital appreciation is typically short-lived.

Just to provide some further insights into its performance, I believe that a comparison against ULTY and YMAX would be appropriate. ULTY operates a similar strategy but with a different set of holdings. YMAX operates as a 'fund of funds' but is still one of the most diversified choices amongst the YieldMax funds. However, I believe that it would only be fair to measure the performance of these funds starting in April 2025, which is when ULTY implemented its strategy change. Looking at the performance comparison below, we can see that LFGY has outperformed both ULTY and YMAX since April.

Since LFGY is focused on crypto businesses, the fund is likely to share some of the sensitivity to shifts in the price of Bitcoin. Since the fund's operating history is so limited, we haven't been able to experience how closely linked LFGY is to Bitcoin. However, I remain cautious about a significant pullback in the price of Bitcoin and how it will negatively impact LFGY's share price and the ability to sustain its current payouts. I believe that the correlation may not be as clear right now because Bitcoin has continued to hover near its highs.

Distribution Utility

According to the latest declared weekly distribution of $0.512 per share, the estimated annual yield sits above 72% at this time. One of the biggest issues I've had with many of the YieldMax funds is that their payouts tend to trend downward over time, especially after their share price has experienced some mild deterioration. However, LFGY differentiates itself by providing shareholders with a very steady range of payouts since the fund's inception. The fund launched shortly before the market's rapid decline back in March, which is why we can see payouts slightly declining at the start of the graph below.

Admittedly, this payout stability can change at any moment, so I would still advise caution. The payouts can shift based on the fund's underlying success with its option strategy or just based on the overall momentum of the market or its holdings. I believe that either a sharp or prolonged period of decline in market indices will result in LFGY's payouts being reduced over time. As the fund's net assets decrease, so will the payouts. This is a trend that has been observed throughout many different YieldMax funds. Although LFGY holds common shares of the underlying equities that it writes options against, I still believe that the payouts can decline.

Despite these risks, investors like myself will always be interested in a fund that offers a distribution rate as high as this. In theory, this large yield provides investors with the possibility of reaching house money status, which is the point where your initially invested capital has been fully repaid through distributions alone. Just to demonstrate this concept, we can use some basic napkin math. Assume that we have $10,000 invested at a 72.5% distribution rate.

$10,000 x 72.5% = an estimated annual income of ~$7,250.

Therefore, investors will be able to eventually collect their initial capital back in a little over a year. The unfortunate part is that it requires a longer holding period to reach this point. Since many of the underlying holdings are extremely volatile and have some sort of indirect exposure to the crypto markets, there's always a chance that LFGY is impacted by a pullback in these riskier assets.

On a more positive note, LFGY typically pays out these large distributions using return of capital. Return of capital distributions aren't considered income and, therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution lowers an investor's cost basis, and this allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. So it is possible to hold a position in LFGY long enough for your cost basis to reach $0 per share. At this point, all of the income received going forward would be classified as long-term capital gains, which are considered to be qualified dividend income and are more tax-efficient.

However, it's worth noting that the distributions aren't always paid out using return of capital. For instance, this week's payout contained no return of capital, which means that investors will see tax consequences from all of the income received here. Therefore, it's important to meticulously track the weekly distributions and how they are being funded, so that you can properly estimate the tax bill at the end of the year. I would highly suggest going to the YieldMax site and using their contact form to request being added to the distribution list.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate LFGY as a buy at this time due to the high-income potential and the fund's ability to keep payouts consistent since its inception. I like the fact that LFGY actually holds common shares of the equities that it writes options against. This helps reduce the risks related to synthetic options, which many other YieldMax funds utilize. Additionally, LFGY implements strategy collars and put spreads to minimize the downside risk. Despite this, investors should still consider the risks of LFGY's crypto-related portfolio of holdings. As Bitcoin remains near its all-time highs, the fund's payouts and performance have been great. However, this consistency may change if the Bitcoin cycle shifts and there's a large pullback.

data.title: 13-Week Money Supply Grows By 3.7% Following Seasonal Trend
data.description:
Money supply is a very important indicator. It helps show how tight or loose current monetary conditions are regardless of what the Fed is doing with interest rates. Even if the Fed is tight, if the money supply is increasing, it has an inflationary effect.

One key metric shown below is the “Wenzel” 13-week annualized money supply figure. It was made popular by the late Robert Wenzel who tracked the metric weekly as an indicator for where the economy might be headed. In 2020, the Fed started reporting the data monthly instead of weekly. It should also be noted that money supply data can be heavily revised in future months.

Recent Trends

Seasonally adjusted money supply has been growing on a consistent monthly basis since January 2024. The latest month (August) showed another solid increase of $80B. This continues the trend of elevated money supply growth.

Figure 1: MoM M2 Change (Seasonally Adjusted)

The increase was 4.4% annualized, which is below the 4.8% over the past year.

Figure 2: M2 Growth Rates

August average is typically around 6.5%, which puts the latest month below average.

Figure 3: Average Monthly Growth Rates

Non-seasonally adjusted shows positive growth for the months of August and September (note: this data is ahead of the seasonally adjusted data).

The weekly data shows what happened. There was a decent pop in the first week of September.

Figure 4: WoW M2 Change

The “Wenzel” 13-week Money Supply

The late Robert Wenzel of Economic Policy Journal used a modified calculation to track money supply. He used a trailing 13-week average growth rate annualized as defined in his book The Fed Flunks.

He specifically used the weekly data that was not seasonally adjusted. His analogy was that in order to know what to wear outside, he wants to know the current weather, not temperatures that have been averaged throughout the year.

The objective of the 13-week average is to smooth some of the choppy data without bringing in too much history that could blind someone from seeing what’s in front of them. The 13-week average growth rate can be seen in the table below.

Growth has started to pick back up again after a slight deceleration through the summer months. Over 7 weeks, money supply growth went from 5.16% to 3.19% before accelerating back up to 3.7%.

Figure 5: WoW Trailing 13-Week Average Money Supply Growth

The plot below shows how this year compares with previous years. This year has started at about the average point over the last 10 years. Growth typically bottoms in August before picking back up to close out the year. The trend this year is almost exactly in the middle of all past trends. It has been almost exactly average on the year.

Figure 6: Yearly 13-Week Overlay

Inflation and Money Supply

The chart below shows the history of inflation, money supply, and Fed Funds. As shown, in 1970 inflation worked with a ~2-year lag compared to money supply. Money supply slowed dramatically in 2023 and 2024 but has been moving back up. Inflation has also been stickier than the Fed would like, but unfortunately, they cannot do much given the large debt load of the US Government and corporations.

Figure 9: YoY M2 Change with CPI and Fed Funds

Historical Perspective

The charts below are designed to put the current trends into historical perspective. The orange bars represent annualized percentage change rather than raw dollar amount.

Figure 8: M2 with Growth Rate

Below shows the 13-week annualized average over history. This chart overlays the log return of the S&P. Mr. Wenzel proposed that large drops in money supply could be a sign of stock market pullbacks. His theory, derived from Murray Rothbard, states that when the market experiences a shrinking growth rate of money supply (or even negative), it can create liquidity issues in the stock market, leading to a sell-off.

While not a perfect predictive tool, many of the dips in money supply precede market dips. Specifically, the major dips in 2002 and 2008 from +10% down to 0%. 2022 was highly correlated with a fall in money supply, and the rebound has corresponded with the big stock market move we saw in 2023 and into 2024. The slowing growth in 2025 did correlate with the market drop in March/April, but that was clearly more headline-driven.

Please note the chart only shows market data through Sep 1st to align with available M2 data.

Figure 9: 13-Week M2 Annualized and S&P 500

One other consideration is the reverse repo market at the Fed. This is a tool that allows financial institutions to swap cash for instruments on the Fed balance sheet.

Reverse repos peaked at $2.55T on Dec 30, 2022. Money gushed out from March 2023 to May 2024. The outflows have taken another leg down as the remaining balances have been drained. This is the lowest level since 2021, now standing at $11B.

Figure 10: Fed Reverse Repurchase Agreements

Wrapping Up

Non-seasonally adjusted 13-week money supply growth has started to pick back up. This is likely the quiet before the storm. Now that Trump is openly meddling with the Fed, it’s only a matter of time before money supply starts moving up quickly, with inflation numbers to follow shortly thereafter.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Global Dividend Growth Accelerates As The Bull Market Turns 3
data.description:
Dividend-increase announcements are on the rise. According to the Wall Street Horizon research team, 71.9% of all dividend changes have been positive so far in 2025. That tops the comparable year-to-date figures of 68.8% and 68.6% from 2024 and 2023, respectively. In all, the current-year hike tally is now 3,553. Perhaps the bump is not surprising, given that the global bull market is about to turn three years old.

Global Dividend Changes: A High Hike-Percentage in 2025

More dividend payouts mean investment teams need to adjust stock prices to reflect these corporate actions properly in order to conduct time-series analysis. TMX’s Price Adjustment Curve (PAC) provides price adjustments applicable down to tick-level prices or even orders, and below you can see the number of recorded adjustments for North American dividends have been steadily increasing. Q2 2025 recorded 11,194 such price adjustments, the largest second quarter tally in our five years of data. With one week left in the current quarter, the Q3 2025 total stands at 8,904.

October also marks the start of the third quarter earnings season. The big banks begin reporting profit numbers on October 14, with JPMorgan Chase (JPM) providing the unofficial kickstart.

Unlike a year ago, however, equity tailwinds are not primarily a U.S. story. With just a handful of trading days left in Q3, the iShares MSCI ACWI ex U.S. ETF (ACWX) boasts an 11.4 percentage-point lead over the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Much of the difference is explained by a 10% year-to-date drop in the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY).

Help from the Dollar

In general, a softer greenback is a profit tailwind for large multinational corporations as they repatriate their overseas sales from foreign currencies to the dollar. Indeed, S&P 500 earnings per share estimates are at fresh highs. It’s against that backdrop that companies, despite the ongoing tariff threat, are willing to boost their dividends. It may leave you and other investors wondering: What could disrupt the steady diet of dividend-hike announcements?

As always, we can put the spotlight on the consumer. August’s Retail Sales report, released last week, confirmed that household spending is resilient, a softening labor market notwithstanding. The so-called “core control group” of the U.S. Census Bureau’s consumption snapshot rose by 0.7% over the middle month of the third quarter, a crucial period for domestic retailers.1 The back-to-school shopping season was apparently a hit—particularly for e-commerce firms.

Jobs Data on Tap

Robust consumer spending may hinge on the employment situation. We’ll know more about that next Friday once the September jobs report crosses the wires. Expectations could be low after back-to-back soft payroll gains, not to mention the steep downward revisions from the annual Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (QCEW) report put out by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics two weeks ago. Further labor market nuggets could be found in next week’s multitude of employment indicators.

According to our Economic Calendar, with data licensed from Econoday Inc., August job vacancy figures via the Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) prints at 10 a.m. ET on September’s final day, the ADP private employment numbers come the following morning, and the now widely followed Challenger Job Cuts survey publishes on Thursday, October 2, in the premarket. Investors shouldn’t discount the importance of the Employment sub index readings within both the ISM’s and S&P Global’s Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) surveys. Those are in next week’s queue, too.

What To Do with All That Free Cash Flow?

How the macro setting unfolds could determine companies’ capital allocation plans heading into 2026. We typically don’t see a high number of dividend-increase or decrease announcements in the second half of the year compared to the first half. A strong finish to 2025, though, and next year’s first quarter sum could eclipse the Q1 2025 dividend-hike tally of 1,803.

And here’s why that’s significant: Investors increasingly prefer that companies return cash flow to them via share buybacks and dividend payments. That was one of the takeaways from BofA’s September Global Fund Manager Survey. Along with increased capital spending, there’s less desire from shareholders for firms to shore up their balance sheets.

Record Buybacks, Record Dividends? JPM In Focus.

So, will that wish come true? With U.S. corporate buybacks reaching $1 trillion in August, we may learn more on the dividend front soon.2 It’s very much a “watch what I do, not what I say” situation.

It’s hard to go a few minutes with financial TV on in the background without your ears perking up due to cautious words from CEOs and other business executives. One of the poster children for cloudy executive outlooks is undoubtedly Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPM. America’s banker routinely expresses trepidation regarding fiscal and other macro matters. Yet guess what big bank announced a significant dividend boost? JPMorgan.

On September 16, the world’s most valuable bank by market cap raised its quarterly dividend from $1.40 to $1.50.3 The press release came after a strong Q2 report issued back in July and the authorization of a new share repurchase program. Now the third-best-performing component of the Dow Jones Industrial Average on the year, JPM may have eyes on the $1 trillion market-cap figure. If it were to do so, it would join fellow Financials-sector stalwart Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) in that exclusive club.

How the Fed Fits In

And here’s something else to consider on the topic of dividends at this juncture of the economic cycle: Given the Fed’s apparent willingness to run the economy hot in terms of inflation, additional rate cuts may be in store not just in Q4, but throughout 2026. If that happens (and Treasury yields hold in check), it could make dividend stocks all the more attractive compared to bonds.

The Bottom Line

Dividend-increase announcements are tracking above year-ago levels and 2023’s pace. Even with shakiness in the labor market and some guarded outlooks offered by companies during the busy Q3 conference season, shareholders are being rewarded with growing quarterly payouts.

We’ll find out more in the weeks ahead—first from next week’s employment numbers, and second as third quarter earnings reports roll in (assuming we are sticking with quarterly updates!).

Citations

  1. S&P 500 eases a bit from record as traders await big Fed rate decision: Live updates, CNBC, Pia Singh, Alex Harring, September 16, 2025.
  2. US Firms Racing Through $1 Trillion Buyback Spree in Record Time, Bloomberg, Joel Leon, August 27, 2025.
  3. JPMorganChase Declares Increased Common Stock Dividend, JPMorganChase, September 16, 2025.

Original Post

data.title: Geely: Undervalued, Moving Forward As One
data.description:
This is my third piece on Geely Auto (OTCPK:GELYF); first was out exactly a year ago (GELYF is up more than 100% since) and second after full 2024 results earlier in March (+12%). This is my strongest conviction so far.

Geely Auto is moving up both at home and abroad, across all portfolios, ICE and NEV, its models packed with more premium tech features than ever before. Competitive pressures persist but parent group synergies, geographic diversification and financial flexibility mitigate the risks. The stock is cheap versus peers and historically.

Financial update

H1 of 2025 looked objectively impressive. First off, sales volume (+46%) reached a new-time high of 1.4 million units. That pushed the company’s domestic market share, for the first time, into double digits. With 10%, Geely Auto is currently in second place, both overall and in the NEV market behind leader BYD (which holds 13% and 30% respectively).

Both top and bottom line metrics rose accordingly: revenue by 27% (CNY150b), gross profit by 24% (CNY25b) and net attributable profit by 102% (CNY6.6b). Cash and equivalents, as a result, grew 68% (CNY69b). Although debt levels also increased — and pretty dramatically, adding nearly CNY15b — the outlook remains stable.

Absolute increases, however, did not lead to improved profitability across the board. Gross margin, for one, stayed put around 16% — understandably so, given intense price competition among Chinese automakers, with (often hidden) incentives and promotions persisting despite government calls for moderation.

Where Geely Auto has managed to shape up is operational efficiency. Administrative, selling and R&D ratios all saw big drops just in the first half of this year. Net margin, consequently, increased from 2.8% to 4.4%, indicating that Geely Auto is edging closer to profitability pace-setter, BYD’s 5% level.

“One Geely”

In the Taizhou Declaration made in September 2024, Zhejiang Geely Holding vowed to pursue ‘deep integration’ throughout its business units in an ultimate effort to improve competitiveness. One year on and things are moving along nicely. The main development to date has been of Geely Auto acquiring Zeekr (which now includes Lynk & Co) by year-end.

The idea is to have Geely Auto — the designated listed vehicle that Zhejiang has been streamlining around — benefit from Zeekr’s position in the premium NEV market, while also helping Zeekr turn over a new leaf by letting it leverage the bigger automaker’s manufacturing scale and technical capabilities.

This entire strategy of pooling resources and eliminating redundancies is what Geely Auto has needed all along. At the moment, the automaker is one of the few Chinese players conservatively reducing its R&D expenditure. But money is hardly a concern given the technological prowess of the holding group — and collaboration is the new name of the game.

Beyond commercial vehicle brands, setting new milestones are Zhejiang’s smart mobility arms. Farizon, for example, is to supply Hong Kong’s first methanol hybrid electric buses, while Aerofugia has introduced a pure electric manned e-VTOL vehicle for low altitude travel. Supporting innovations in mobility is the group’s satellite operator GeeSpace whose infrastructure may in future bring unique value to Geely Auto as well.

Product update

I mentioned this before, Geely Auto’s biggest strength lies in the breadth of its offerings. It had already been an ICE leader in China (still is) but now it has also become a top player in NEV. The Galaxy series, released last year, is going strong: in 1H, sales were up by over 230% hitting 548,000 units. NEV overall finally made up more than half (52%) of total sales.

New models — over 10 in 2025, many hybrid plug-ins — are being rolled out to good reviews. Global reach keeps expanding, in terms of both sales network and manufacturing presence, in regions such as the Middle East, Asia Pacific, Latin America, Europe and Central Asia, where the company is already established.

One area where Geely Auto is catching up fast with top competition is software and electrified platforms. It is scaling AI driving (G-Pilot) and cockpit (Flyme Auto), safer and faster‑charging batteries (Aegis short blade LFP and 900V e‑drive), high‑efficiency hybrids (NordThor), backed by big computing power (23.5 EFLOPS). These features are due to become standard across most models.

Geely Auto is making sure to learn from the best. Leading peers are following similar trajectories, loading vehicles with advanced tech and diversifying to foreign shores. Geely’s G-Pilot, for example, is a direct analogue of BYD’s God’s Eye and Tesla’s FSD positioning in China, while its portfolio‑wide ADAS deployment mirrors BYD’s extending affordable ADAS even to low‑price segments.

BYD exported close to 500,000 units in 1H 2025 (up 230% year-on-year), more than 20% of total sales. Geely Auto's growth rates are matching: the NEV portion of its 184,000 exported units, making up 13% of total sales, posted a growth rate of 307%. Chinese plug-in hybrids are proving particularly popular, validating Geely Auto's export focus on both PHEVs and BEVs.

Risks

Price wars among Chinese automakers are in now in third consecutive year. This ‘involution’ will act as a natural selection mechanism of sorts, drastically reducing the number of brands (standing at 170 at present, 129 of which are pure NEV) over the next several years. Geely Auto — as an efficient, vertically integrated, diversified business — will endure.

Export markets offer relief. But these too are exposed to risk in the form of trade restrictions. The biggest impact will be on Geely Auto’s European sales; the extra tariff is around 19% (vs. 17% for BYD and about 8% for Tesla). The company does make some use of Volvo’s EU manufacturing facilities; efforts to set up own production base are underway.

Conclusion

Geely Auto’s transformation is picking up pace. The full-year sales target has been updated from 2.7 million to 3 million units. Cost control is yielding margin gains despite pricing pressures. Integrating ever more brands does add to operational complexity but there is a proven track record in execution. The holding company provides a unique edge in satellite connectivity and synergies with the rest of the group. All in all, Geely Auto is a top contender across powertrain options, in China and beyond. The fact that it remains underrated — and hence undervalued (P/E 11x for HK.0175) — is a clear signal to Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Evolution Of The S&P/BMV IPC Ecosystem
data.description:
By Eduardo Olazabal

Since its inception in 1978, the S&P/BMV IPC has served as Mexico’s flagship equity index, tracking the performance of the 35 largest and most liquid stocks listed on the Bolsa Mexicana de Valores (BMV). Over the decades, the index has become a cornerstone of the Mexican financial market, and the ecosystem surrounding it has grown substantially. Today, there are more than USD 9 billion in assets benchmarked or tracking the index, including Latin America’s largest locally listed ETF, which manages over USD 4.5 billion in assets.1

An Expanding Ecosystem of Indices

The S&P/BMV IPC has not only served as a benchmark but also as the foundation for a wide range of derived indices. These indices cater to diverse investment strategies and preferences, enabling market participants to track Mexican equities in more targeted and innovative ways.

Global Accessibility and Market Integration

The S&P/BMV IPC is the most liquid and representative equity index in Mexico, making it a natural candidate for derivatives trading. Historically, futures and options linked to the index have been listed on MexDer, providing market participants with tools for hedging and exposure to Mexican equities.

The recent launch of e-mini S&P/BMV IPC futures on CME represents an expansion of this ecosystem, providing market participants with more options for access to the Mexican equity market. This milestone reflects the continued evolution of the S&P/BMV IPC ecosystem, which has been built over the years through robust infrastructure, regulatory alignment and market education.

Now, investors around the world can access the Mexican equity market nearly 24 hours a day, which may support:

  • Real-time risk management across time zones;
  • Capital allocation across regions and asset classes; and
  • Using margining frameworks that apply across multiple equity index products.

Moreover, the availability of these futures is expected to enhance cross-market participation and improve price discovery, ultimately contributing to greater liquidity in the Mexican equity market.

Unlocking New Opportunities

With the global availability of futures, market participants around the world have new tools to:

  • Hedge Mexican equity exposure;
  • Express tactical views on Mexico within global portfolios; and
  • Access one of the largest emerging equity markets.

As the S&P/BMV IPC ecosystem continues to evolve, these developments represent more than just new products—they symbolize a gateway to deeper liquidity, broader participation, and greater integration of Mexico into the global investment landscape.

Footnote

1 - Data from Morningstar as of Aug. 29, 2025.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Visa: The Price Isn't Right
data.description:
With a market capitalization of $655.1 billion, Visa (NYSE:V) is not just a massive player in the financial industry. It's also one of the largest publicly traded companies on the planet. Over time, the business has grown rather rapidly, with revenue, profits, and cash flows expanding meaningfully. But ever since I started writing about the company in November 2023, I have been "Neutral" on it because of its valuation. The overall crux of my argument is that while it is a high-quality business that will continue to grow, shares are frankly too expensive to justify market beating potential.

Since I last reaffirmed the company as a "Hold" candidate in June of this year, in an article in which I discussed the ways that it and its shareholders should benefit from stablecoin’s imminent rise, shares have dipped by 1.3%. That's far worse than the 8.9% rise enjoyed by the S&P 500 over the same period. However, since I originally rated it a "Hold" in November 2023, the stock is up 37.9%, which is not terribly far away from the 44.9% rise that the broader market has experienced. Looking at the data today, I continue to see attractive growth for the firm. But again, that does nothing to change the reality of its valuation. Although I believe the company has significant growth ahead, I think taking a more cautious approach and maintaining my "Hold" rating is the right choice.

A great business at a not-so-great price

The newest data regarding Visa covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue came in at $10.17 billion. That represents an increase of 14.3% over the $8.90 billion the company reported one year earlier. Rising revenue for the company is tied to the overall growth in the number of transactions the company can process. According to management, the business benefited from a rise in the number of cards that it has outstanding. Last year, during the third quarter, the firm had 4.5 billion cards outstanding. Today, that number is even higher at 4.8 billion.

To put this in perspective, we need only look at the chart above. In it, you can see how other major players in the payment processing industry stack up. Mastercard (MA), for instance, had about 3.2 billion cards outstanding as of the end of its most recent quarter. That was up from the 3 billion that it had one year earlier. By comparison, American Express (AXP) is tiny. The company has only 149.4 million cards. But that is up nicely from the 144.3 million that it had at the same time last year. When we break out the math, we can see that the market share that Visa has in relation to these two competitors is 58.55%. This is, unfortunately, a bit lower than the 58.79% that it reported a year earlier. But still, that makes it a giant in its space.

The overall increase in the number of cards the company has is tied to the overall volumes that it processes. During the quarter, if we include cash volumes of $599 billion, the company processed $3.94 trillion worth of transactions. That represents a rise of 4.3% compared to the $3.78 trillion the company reported at the same time last year. For context, volumes processed by Mastercard amounted to $2.18 trillion, which was up from the $1.97 trillion reported the previous year. American Express, meanwhile, grew from $440.6 billion to $472 billion. Unfortunately, these numbers mean that Visa did experience a decline in its share between these three firms, with that figure dropping from 61.03% to 59.77%. But again, that still makes it the big player between the three. It is worth noting that the volume of transactions grew because the total number of transactions jumped nicely, surging 10.3% from 59.32 billion to 65.44 billion.

It is important to note that while payment volumes play a role in the revenue the company generates, there are other factors as well. As an example, the business provides certain data processing services. This includes revenue that it gets from authorization, clearing, and settlement activities. It provides value-added services involved with the issuing, acceptance, and risk and identity activities of other firms, and it even generates maintenance and support services revenue. This was a big contributor to the company's revenue growth, with sales jumping 14.8% from $4.49 billion to $5.15 billion. According to management, this was because of process transactions jumping 10% on a year-over-year basis.

Touching briefly once more on the transaction side, I would like to point out that a disproportionate share of its revenue growth came from its international transaction activities. In fact, year over year, revenue for this part of the business expanded by 13.7%, from $3.19 billion to $3.63 billion. This growth was because of a 13% rise in cross-border volumes. On top of this, other miscellaneous revenue for the company grew nicely from $780 million to $1.03 billion. This was thanks to greater demand for its advisory services, price changes that the company implemented, and more.

As revenue for the company grew, profitability expanded as well. Net income jumped from $4.87 billion to $5.27 billion. But that's not all. We also saw an expansion in operating cash flow, with that metric rising from $5.13 billion to $6.73 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $9 billion to $10.26 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company grew from $6.10 billion to $7.11 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. As was the case in the third quarter on its own, the company saw a rise in revenue, profits, and cash flows. As the number of cards that the business had increased, the volume of transactions grew, jumping from $11.54 trillion to $12.13 trillion, and as the company benefited from an 11.4% jump in data processing revenue, top-line and bottom-line results improved markedly. The rise in transaction volume was made possible by an increase in the number of transactions from 172.25 billion to 189.89 billion.

For the current fiscal year, management expects earnings per share to rise at a low teens rate. Based on my own estimates, if we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would get net profits of around $20.49 billion. Following the same approach would get us adjusted operating cash flow of about $23.40 billion and EBITDA of $28.05 billion. If we use these estimates and the historical results that the company achieved last year, we would be able to see in the chart below how the company is currently valued. Honestly, these are rather lofty multiples. But again, this is a high-quality company that's growing nicely. So some premium would typically be justified. Even with that factored in, however, I would say that the stock is anything but cheap. It would even be difficult to call it affordable.

Relative to the other two competitors that I brought up already, Visa is neither expensive nor cheap. In the same chart, you can see how the business stacks up against both of these firms. It is important to note, however, that the operational structure of American Express makes evaluating the company on an EV-to-EBITDA basis not really possible. So it is excluded from that metric.

Even though Visa is not a cheap firm, it is worth noting that management has a good track record of buying back stock. In the image below, you can see that, in the most recent quarter, it repurchased 14 million shares on the open market. This does represent a drop from the 17 million reported the previous year. But the overall dollar amount of repurchases grew from $4.78 billion to $4.83 billion because of a rise in the average cost per share from $276.75 to $349.24.

Thanks to these repurchases, the total number of shares repurchased so far this year totals 40 million at a cost of $13.24 billion. That compares to the 42 million shares bought back at the same time last year at a cost to shareholders of $11.16 billion. Considering how the stock is currently priced, I would prefer that management focus more on growth initiatives or even entertain the possibility of additional dividends over the $3.49 billion in dividends paid out during the first nine months of this year. But at least the business is in a position to allocate a large amount of capital to shareholders while achieving attractive growth. So I can't complain too much.

Takeaway

From a purely fundamental standpoint, I do believe that Visa is a solid business with a bright future. But this does not mean that I'm bullish about the firm. To me, shares look very expensive. I wouldn't say that they are so expensive that they would justify a bearish assessment. But as things stand, I believe rating Visa a "Hold" is appropriate.

data.title: Lovesac: A Terrible Deal As Profit Margins Wane (Downgrade)
data.description:
Stock markets are starting to fall back from all-time highs, which is finally a truer reflection of the fact that the macroeconomy is quite weak. Nowhere is that more honestly reflected than in the weak comp sales in retail companies, reflecting a pullback in consumer spending.

Lovesac (NASDAQ:LOVE), the maker of sectional furniture, has been navigating this tough environment for quite some time. And though the company offered a glimmer of hope with comp sales reaccelerating last quarter, the company's recent Q2 print confirmed that strength earlier in the year was more of a fluke. With shares down ~30% year-to-date, Lovesac has further to fall as its profit estimates come down.

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on Lovesac in June, when the stock was still trading at ~$19 per share. Since then, Lovesac has experienced gross margin degradation, while early strength from the company's launch of a new category (couches) has waned. Amid a lower profit outlook for this year, I'm resetting my outlook on Lovesac back down to a "Sell."

The first factor we have to address is Lovesac's guidance cut. Citing wider uncertainty in a shaky tariff environment plus unstable sales trends, the company narrowed its revenue guidance range to $710-$740 million (4%-9% growth), versus a wider 3%-10% range previously. But alongside this, the company also slashed adjusted EBITDA expectations to $42-$55 million, the $48.5 million midpoint of which reflects a 6.7% margin and a 10% reduction from the midpoint of a previous $48-$60 million range.

To me, Lovesac's fall in share price since then has not fully compensated us for the greater operating risk. At current post-earnings share prices near $17, Lovesac trades at a market cap of $256.1 million. After we net off the $34.2 million of cash on Lovesac's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $221.9 million.

This reflects a 4.6x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA multiple. Yes, at face value, Lovesac appears dirt cheap. But there's tremendous execution risk behind that discount. In particular, I'm mindful of the following risks:

  • Lovesac's gross margin strengths appear to be waning with higher promotional discounts. To spur demand, Lovesac has had to turn to a higher rate of promotions, which is eating into the company's historically high gross margin premiums.
  • Limited liquidity. The company has only ~$30 million of cash remaining on its books. It is relying heavily on new showrooms to broaden its product visibility, and it has limited capital with which to expand.
  • Weak comp sales. Despite having easy comps in FY24 when revenue declined, the company is barely posting above flat sales growth, indicating still-weak demand for the furniture category.

I don't think Lovesac is a throwaway just yet, but I don't see any near-term catalysts that can push the stock higher - and I believe we'll have a better opportunity to buy the stock at a lower price down the line. For now, move back to the sidelines and invest elsewhere.

Q2 Download

Let's now go through Lovesac's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Lovesac's revenue of $160.5 million grew 2.5% y/y and was essentially in line with Wall Street's expectations of $160.4 million. That said, we note that Lovesac's growth decelerated meaningfully versus 4.8% y/y growth in Q1.

One of Lovesac's principal growth strategies is expanding its store footprint, which is critical now as the company recently ended its showrooms inside of Best Buy (BBY) stores. The company's total number of showrooms at the end of Q2 was 270, up 6% from 254 in the year-ago quarter.

Without this sales showroom expansion, Lovesac's growth would have been minimal. One core way to judge this is via the company's omnichannel comp sales, which excludes the contribution from newly opened (and recently closed) stores - this clocked in at just 0.9% in the quarter, 190 bps weaker than 2.8% comps growth in Q1.

Interestingly, Lovesac is undergoing a significant refresh of its branding and channel strategy. It's aiming to find new channels to distribute products, ostensibly to replace the loss of outlets like Best Buy. In doing this, the company quickly rebranded its EverCouch product to "Snugg."

Per CEO Shawn Nelson's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

More to come on that, but yes, we see significant new channel opportunities, particularly with some of the new products that we are close to announcing that are still in the living room space. Meanwhile, to better align with this new product and channel strategy, we have chosen to rename the EverCouch product line to be called Snugg by Lovesac. The advertising went live this week with a fresh new look and feel as you will likely see on TV and digital platforms over the next few weeks.

It suits the product better as the Snugg product line, consisting of the Snugg sofa, the Snugg Love seat and the Snugg chair is everything that Lovesac has to offer. It's washable, upgradable, shippable, movable, snugly and comfortable, but in a bit smaller package that can always fit any space and look forever new. We're excited about its performance to date and its rollout recently expanded to 100 of our physical locations already. We promised to share in more detail the results of our brand evolution work, our product road map and hierarchy and channel strategy over the coming quarters as we bring incremental elements to life.

To me, the quick rebrand is a potential signal that sales are struggling, and while we give Lovesac credit for trying to broaden its sales networks, we shouldn't count any chickens before they hatch.

The other key concern is on margins. Lovesac's gross margins are still quite lofty, giving the company ample room to play with pricing. And yet we're concerned about the fact that it's falling, down 260 bps y/y to 56.4%. Note that Lovesac still maintains about a 10-point premium over other higher-end furniture labels like RH (RH) and Williams-Sonoma (WSM), but 100 bps of this margin decay was driven by higher promotions - a signal that Lovesac can only achieve roughly flat comps growth despite heavier investment into pricing.

Owing to tepid top-line results and gross margin decay, Lovesac's already weak adjusted EBITDA fell by nearly half on a y/y basis to just $0.8 million.

Q2 is a seasonally slower quarter for Lovesac, but we worry about the fact that the company may be latching onto a new baseline of weak sales and margin decay that will make its goal of hitting even ~$50 million in annual EBITDA challenging. To be sure, Lovesac is getting little support from the macroeconomy, and though the company's high-50%s margin gives it some room to maneuver on pricing, we're skeptical about a small brand like Lovesac's ability to compete with much more recognizable brands like Pottery Barn (Williams-Sonoma's largest label, generating nearly half of its revenue), Crate & Barrel, and RH amid industry declines.

Key Takeaways

To me, Lovesac is dead money in the short to medium term as the company works through its profit declines. We note that its relatively tight liquidity base (just over $30 million of cash, which is less than a fifth of quarterly revenue) limits Lovesac's options as it struggles with waning margins and adjusted EBITDA and faces deeper-pocketed competitors.

Steer clear here - while I support rotating away from large caps and into undervalued small caps as the overall stock market recedes, Lovesac is a value trap best avoided for now.
